title: The Trolley Problem
definition: A thought experiment in ethics: a runaway trolley will kill five people unless you pull a lever to divert it onto a side track where it will kill one person. Is it morally permissible to pull the lever? The problem explores utilitarian vs. deontological ethics.

proofs.description: Demonstrates conflict between utilitarianism (save the greater number) and deontology (do not intentionally kill)
proofs.reference: Foot, P. (1967). The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of the Double Effect. Oxford Review, 5, 5-15.

proofs.description: Experimental moral psychology studies show variations in responses based on cultural and neurological factors
proofs.reference: Greene, J.D., et al. (2001). An fMRI investigation of emotional engagement in moral judgment. Science, 293(5537), 2105-2108.

difficulty_levels.beginner: A trolley is going to kill five people. You can pull a lever to kill one instead. Do you pull it?
difficulty_levels.intermediate: The trolley problem tests moral intuitions about killing vs. letting die, intention vs. foresight, and agent-relative obligations. Most people say pull the lever, but refuse to push a fat man off a bridge to save five.
difficulty_levels.expert: The problem exposes tensions between consequentialist (maximize lives) and deontological (do not use persons as means) frameworks. Variations explore moral distance, personal force, and dual-process psychology. Practical applications include autonomous vehicle programming and triage ethics.

extra_fields.historical_context: Originated by Philippa Foot (1967), extensively developed by Judith Jarvis Thomson (1976, 1985).
extra_fields.limitations: Abstract; critics argue real-world moral decisions involve more factors. Some question relevance to actual ethics.
